storm.summary_layperson: No thunderstorms expected this evening — a storm complex tracking south from upstate New York is forecast to fall apart well before it reaches the coast.
storm.local_effect_blurb: Long Island Sound's cool water (69°F vs. 82°F inland air) has built a moderate marine stable layer that tends to choke low-level convection at the coastline.
storm.detail_html:
The regional storm picture is quiet for this evening. HRRR puts available CAPE at 60 J/kg — essentially no thermodynamic instability for thunderstorm development near the coast. A weakening MCS tracking south from upstate New York is the main item to watch; NWS forecasters at OKX expect it to largely dissipate before reaching the region as instability is limited locally, with any remnant activity confined to the northern half of the coverage area well north of Riverside.

Bulk shear is elevated at 40 kt (0–6 km, NNW) — the kind of shear that normally supports organized convection. With CAPE at 60 J/kg, however, there is no updraft energy for shear to work with. Note that the RAP model shows a much higher estimate (710 J/kg) at the same forecast hour; this disagreement is flagged in caveats, but HRRR's finer 3-km resolution and the NWS OKX AFD text both support the low-instability read. The more dangerous setup arrives later in the week: scattered thunderstorms are possible Wednesday through Saturday as the upper pattern shifts.

The Riverside cove's protective mechanism is active. Sound water at 69°F versus 82°F inland air creates a +13°F temperature delta and a moderate marine stable layer over the Sound. This layer tends to choke low-level inflow from approaching convection — often weakening or splitting storm cells at the coastline while areas 2–3 miles inland take the full impact. With CAPE this low, any convective remnant from the northern MCS would have minimal energy to begin with; the marine layer suppresses what little remains. The cove is well-insulated from tonight's minimal storm risk.

Active NWS alerts: an Air Quality Alert through tonight (ozone and particulates, heat-driven) and an Extreme Heat Warning beginning tomorrow midday through July 3. Neither is a convective hazard today. What would change the call: an unexpected afternoon pop-up from intense surface heating — possible in theory with inland temps near 90°F — but the OKX AFD assessment and very low HRRR CAPE together make this unlikely before Wednesday.

wind.tactics_blurb: SSW sea breeze at peak (Regime A+) — right side of the course favored as the gradient reinforces the thermal and channels cleanly along the western Belle Haven shore. Start mid-line and work right on the first beat; expect oscillations around 210° through the 17:00–19:00 peak before the thermal softens toward sunset.
wind.tactics_detail_html:
Regime A+ confirmed. Both sea-breeze ingredients are firmly in place: temperature differential (inland 82°F, Sound water 69°F) is 13°F — well above the 5°F threshold — and the synoptic gradient is onshore at 10 kt SSW, reinforcing the thermal rather than opposing it. This sub-regime delivers steadier, stronger wind than a pure-thermal day and holds the flow 30–60 minutes past sunset as the gradient persists once thermal forcing collapses.

HRRR forecasts SSW 197° for the 17:00 window. The western-LIS station ensemble currently observes SSW 203° — a +6° divergence below the 15° override threshold, so trust-obs-over-model is not flagged. The sea-breeze maturation correction applies (+15°, scaled to the 13°F deltaT), placing the corrected forecast direction at 212° SSW for the race window. Direction confidence is high; western-LIS zone direction agreement is 0.90 across 10 stations. Note that KBDR and KHVN anemometers are both offline, degrading the CT coastal METAR wind read — the call relies on RYC, the APRS coastal cluster, and the buoy (stale at 75 min).

In SSW–SW flow at 212°, Greenwich Cove's north–south axis is near-optimally aligned with the inflow. Wind enters cleanly from the Sound with slight channeling along the western shore (Belle Haven / Indian Harbor side). The right side of the course is favored on the beat — starboard tack carries boats toward the west where pressure is marginally stronger and flow is steadier. The east side near Tod's Point sits in a subtle fetch shadow from the Greenwich Point peninsula, running slightly lighter and shiftier.

HRRR shows the peak at 17:00–18:00 (~12 kt), then a steady softening through 19:00–20:00 (8→6 kt) as the thermal relaxes ahead of the 20:22 EDT sunset. In Regime A+, the gradient remnant carries the flow 30–60 minutes past sunset — expect 4 kt at 21:00 and ~4 kt at 22:00, enough for racing but firmly drifter territory. Shift character through the peak hours (17:00–19:00) is mature oscillating around 210–212°; play the lifts and headers rather than committing to a persistent tack.

RYC currently reads SW 3 kt, 10-min average 4.9 kt, gust 12 kt — lighter than the open Sound, typical as the SB matures. GW2598 at Rye (9 mi WSW, directly upwind in SSW flow) is at SSW 7 kt gusting 17, and LI south-shore airports show 11–13 kt SSW. Expect the cove to fill in through the 17:00–18:00 window as over-Sound pressure propagates north. Fleet impact: 12 kt is nicely powered for most one-design dinghies — a good race evening if the breeze arrives on schedule.

data_caveats: KBDR anemometer offline — CT coastal METAR wind unavailable; KGON and APRS coastal cluster used as fallback
KHVN anemometer offline — New Haven coastal wind unavailable
Buoy 44040 MET observation stale (75 min old); SST observation stale (88 min old)
HRRR CAPE (60 J/kg) and RAP CAPE (710 J/kg) disagree substantially — HRRR f01 used as primary; NWS OKX AFD supports the lower estimate
